text:
Heinrich Bullinger’s *De testamento* of 1534 is his major theological and historical-biblical contribution to Reformation thought in its early years. A wide range of related issues are dealt with, including justification by faith, divine accommodation of the human situation in revelation, and the central place of the covenant in Scripture. Bullinger then expands on this Scriptural theme by discussing the original covenant with Adam, the unity of the Old and New Testaments, the meaning of the “seed of Abraham,” and the relation of the covenant to ethical living. In addition, Bullinger discusses the role of the Holy Spirit, the Law, the stipulations and commands of the covenant, and the sacraments as signs of the covenant.

In place of being seen as a specialist treatise on one doctrine, De testamento makes Bullinger out to be a thoroughgoing biblical theologian who sought to call the elect to living holy lives before the Lord God El Shaddai. A philological examination of Bullinger’s Latin use of testaments terminology, that is, the words foedus, testamentum, and pactum, reveals that even though Bullinger may have used the terms technically as synonyms, he obviously approached the use of the word pactum with some caution and therefore in order to avoid any possible trace of a medieval pactum-theology in the presentation of his doctrine. In any case Bullinger’s determination in maintaining a consistent terminology, while showing independence of his Zurich colleague Zwingli, at the same time situates him as the major innovator of covenant theology in the Reformed tradition.
